summary: Gruber reviews Openvibe, a multi-social-network client that combines Bluesky, Mastodon, Threads, and Nostr into one timeline. He finds the concept fundamentally flawed, arguing that mashing different social networks together creates confusion rather than simplicity. Threads integration barely works, the app itself is poorly designed with jarring UI issues like auto-scrolling timelines, and the claim that it appeals to newcomers is nonsensical. He contrasts Openvibe unfavorably with Croissant (cross-posting only) and Ivory (discrete per-account timelines), which he sees as solving actual user problems. His core argument is that different social networks have different cultures and features, and pretending they're one unified platform doesn't work.
key_points: Combining multiple social network timelines into one feed sounds simpler but is actually more cacophonous and confusing in practice
The claim that unified multi-network clients appeal to newcomers is backwards — casual users struggle even with the concept of third-party clients for a single network
Threads integration is effectively broken or write-only, and the app doesn't explain why
The app has serious UX problems including auto-scrolling timelines, slow reloading, low-contrast design, and borderline useless notifications
Cross-posting tools like Croissant and per-account switching like Ivory solve real problems; unified timelines do not
money_quotes: Mashing them all together in one timeline isn't simpler. It sounds simpler but in practice it's more cacophonous.
Pretending that they all form one big (lowercase-m) meta platform doesn't make that pretense cohesive.

The idea of explaining to them why they might want to use an app named Openvibe to access Mastodon, Bluesky, and Threads (and the weirdo blockchain network Nostr) is like trying to explain to your dog why they should stay out of the trash.
The concept of streaming multiple accounts from multiple networks into one timeline is by definition a bit advanced.
It's like having a book turn the page automatically while you're reading in the middle of a page.
The overall look, feel, and layout says 'generic Twitter-style social network'.
key_insight: Different social networks have distinct cultures and features, and combining them into a single timeline creates confusion rather than the simplicity it promises.
